records.ety: [F. cours, course, L. cursus, fr. currere to run. See Current.]

defs: The act of moving from one point to another; progress; passage.
quotes: And when we had finished our course from Tyre, we came to Ptolemais.

defs: The ground or path traversed; track; way.
quotes: The same horse also run the round course at Newmarket.

defs: Motion, considered as to its general or resultant direction or to its goal; line progress or advance.
quotes: A light by which the Argive squadron steers Their silent course to Ilium's well known shore.
Westward the course of empire takes its way.

defs: Progress from point to point without change of direction; any part of a progress from one place to another, which is in a straight line, or on one direction; as, a ship in a long voyage makes many courses; a course measured by a surveyor between two stations; also, a progress without interruption or rest; a heat; as, one course of a race.
Motion considered with reference to manner; or duly progress; procedure in a certain line of thought or action; as, the course of an argument.
quotes: The course of true love never did run smooth.

defs: Customary or established sequence of events; recurrence of events according to natural laws.
quotes: By course of nature and of law.
Day and night, Seedtime and harvest, heat and hoary frost, Shall hold their course.

defs: Method of procedure; manner or way of conducting; conduct; behavior.
quotes: My lord of York commends the plot and the general course of the action.
By perseverance in the course prescribed.
You hold your course without remorse.

defs: A series of motions or acts arranged in order; a succession of acts or practices connectedly followed; as, a course of medicine; a course of lectures on chemistry.
The succession of one to another in office or duty; order; turn.
quotes: He appointed . . . the courses of the priests

defs: That part of a meal served at one time, with its accompaniments.
quotes: He [Goldsmith] wore fine clothes, gave dinners of several courses, paid court to venal beauties.

defs: A continuous level range of brick or stones of the same height throughout the face or faces of a building.
The lowest sail on any mast of a square-rigged vessel; as, the fore course, main course, etc.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Coursed (k?rst)); p. pr. & vb. n. Coursing.]

defs: To run, hunt, or chase after; to follow hard upon; to pursue.
quotes: We coursed him at the heels.

defs: To cause to chase after or pursue game; as, to course greyhounds after deer.

quotes: The bounding steed courses the dusty plain.

defs: To run as in a race, or in hunting; to pursue the sport of coursing; as, the sportsmen coursed over the flats of Lancashire.
To move with speed; to race; as, the blood courses through the veins.
